trajectory_summary: This call collapses at the interest-in-solar question when Cecilia reveals she already has an existing solar system and is looking for a replacement refrigerator — not a new install. Holland correctly identifies the mismatch but exits immediately without running any orphaned-flow probe. The call ends with a list removal before it's established whether Cecilia's existing system creates a qualifying opportunity or a hard DQ. The trajectory is a premature exit, not a worked lead.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the call should not be tagged as a clean DQ or a worked lead. Holland exited before the orphaned flow questions could determine whether a qualifying opportunity exists. Cecilia has an existing solar system — orphaned flow protocol requires probing ownership (own vs. lease/PPA), installer identity, system status, and size before a DQ decision is valid. The exit was premature; whether the underlying opportunity would have survived orphaned flow is unconfirmed.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Well, I do have a solar, that runs, the reason why I wanted was asking because I have a solar, and when the solar was brought to me, the solar on it says, Sacred Power.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland exits the call without running a single orphaned-flow question. 'Sacred Power' is not on Ion's hard-DQ installer list, and the ownership/status questions (own vs. lease, system working, system size) take under 60 seconds. The call ends as a list removal when it should have entered the orphaned-flow sequence. This is the only coaching beat that matters for this call — everything else is secondary.

key_moments.quote: You were looking for a solar refrigerator?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland correctly identifies that Ion doesn't sell solar refrigerators — that's accurate. But she treats the refrigerator ask as the entirety of Cecilia's situation rather than as a surface signal sitting on top of an existing solar installation. The knowledge gap is not about refrigerators; it's about recognizing that 'I already have solar' is the orphaned-flow trigger, not a DQ. Reactive read holds: Holland didn't recognize the trigger and exited on the wrong variable.

key_moments.quote: I'll give you off our list, though, but you have a good rest of your day.
key_moments.stewart_read: Cecilia has just spent 45 seconds describing a corroding refrigerator that came with a solar system she received — a disclosure that suggests she may have limited resources and is dealing with a real appliance problem. Holland's exit is abrupt and transactional: list removal, no acknowledgment of what Cecilia shared, no human close. The 'Pardon me?' at 04:05 confirms the exit landed without warning. This is an exit-quality failure, not a vulnerability-disclosure trigger — Cecilia made no explicit vulnerability disclosure — but the abruptness is still a coaching gap on basic call closure.

key_moments.quote: I have you, Cecilia, down as the property owner. Is there anybody else in the title of the home with you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland correctly executes the title co-owner check per the verify protocol. This is a positive execution moment — the question is asked cleanly, in the right place in the sequence, and Cecilia's 'No' is confirmed. Worth noting for coaching balance: Holland was running the verify section competently before the call collapsed at the interest question.

key_moments.quote: And then is your roof just a regular asphalt shingle roof?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland correctly captures roof type and follows up with roof age at 01:57. The roof age answer is complex — Cecilia describes multiple sections with different ages, the oldest dating to '95 or '98 — which is a potential DQ flag (roof over 15 years). Holland acknowledges it and moves on without flagging it or probing for replacement plans. Given the call ends before this matters, the roof age is a secondary note, but the cross-sell signal (old roof → roofing conversation) was available and not touched.
